nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on steep outer reef slopes and outer channel walls at depths of about 0–65 m (most commonly 0–15 m), hovering close to coral or rubble at the base of dropoffs either solitary or in small loosely scattered groups.

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding primarily on small benthic crustaceans. In captivity generally adjusts readily to prepared aquarium foods including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped krill / squid / clam / mussel, and quality carnivore-formulated marine pellet or frozen preparations. A varied, pigment-rich diet is commonly considered helpful in maintaining the bright magenta coloration, which has been reported to fade on monotonous diets. Generally advised to feed one to two small meals per day.

summary: A small, brilliantly magenta-purple dottyback from the western Pacific, generally considered hardy and beginner-accessible but notably semi-aggressive toward similar-shaped tankmates. Best kept singly in a reef tank with abundant rockwork and a tight lid.
careNotes:
The Purple Dottyback (widely sold as Magenta or Strawberry Dottyback) is one of the more commonly kept small dottybacks in the marine trade. Taxonomic note: the species was reclassified from Pseudochromis porphyreus to Pictichromis porphyrea (Lubbock & Goldman, 1974), but the legacy genus is still in widespread use by retailers (including LiveAquaria) — both names refer to the same fish. It is generally considered hardy, readily accepts captive foods, and tolerates the standard reef parameter window.

Despite its small size and unassuming appearance, the species is reliably reported to be semi-aggressive and territorial: while not as belligerent as some Pseudochromis congeners, it is described by hobby sources as 'still all dottyback' and prone to harassing tankmates that are similar in shape, size, or color, especially peaceful species added after it. Conspecific aggression is high — keep one per tank, and do not combine with other dottybacks or similar-shaped fish. Generally advised to be added last to a community tank.

Reef compatibility is generally considered good with corals and most sessile invertebrates, but the species has been reported to prey on small ornamental shrimp; larger cleaner / fire shrimp typically coexist safely.

Jumping is a documented risk — multiple sources note specimens escaping through gaps in aquarium covers, so a tight-fitting lid is generally advised. Plenty of live rock with caves, crevices, and overhangs is required to give it a defendable home territory.

Cross-source spread: max size — LiveAquaria 3 in (~7.6 cm), BlueZoo 2.5 in (~6.4 cm), FishBase 6.0 cm TL, Fishkeeper.co.uk 6 cm; averaged to 5–7 cm. Temperature — LiveAquaria 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), FishBase wild 25.5–29.4°C, Fishkeeper.co.uk 22–28°C; averaged to 22–27°C. pH and salinity universally 8.1–8.4 and 1.020–1.025 SG. Minimum tank — LiveAquaria 30 US gal (~114 L), BlueZoo 24 gal (~91 L), Reefbuilders 20–30 gal; biased upward to a nice 100 L round number to provide adequate territory in light of the species' territorial behavior. Lifespan is not stated by the primary retail or FishBase entries; the 5–8 year range used here is reported by saltwaterfish.com and is consistent with general Pseudochromidae longevity.

Distinguishing note from the Orchid Dottyback (Pictichromis fridmani): the Purple/Magenta Dottyback lacks the black eye stripe and dark-purple fin edging of the Orchid, appearing more uniformly bright magenta.
breedingNotes: Spawning has been reported in home aquariums, though larval rearing is generally considered extremely challenging due to the small larval size. Like other dottybacks, the male is generally expected to prepare a cave or crevice and court a female, who deposits an egg ball that the male typically guards. Mature females may appear visibly fuller-bodied than males. Commercial captive-bred specimens are produced in limited numbers and are sometimes available, though wild-caught fish dominate the trade and are reported to be similarly hardy. Home-aquarium pair formation is impractical in normal community tanks because conspecifics cannot be safely combined in modest volumes.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for confirmed common/scientific name (Pseudochromis porphyreus — legacy genus still used by retailer; current valid name is Pictichromis porphyrea per FishBase), family Pseudochromidae, care level Easy, temperament Semi-Aggressive, reef-safe Yes, diet carnivore, max size 3 in, minimum tank 30 US gal, water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), and the warning about aggression toward other Pseudochromis or similarly shaped fish. Reached via the /collections/dottybacks index per the manifest.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Secondary source — confirmed Pseudochromis porphyreus / Pictichromis porphyrea, Indo-Pacific origin, max size 2.5 in, minimum tank 24 US gal, reef-compatible, beginner care level, semi-aggressive temperament, carnivorous diet (krill, shrimp, squid, clam, mussel), strong caution that the species 'can often be quite the bullies' and is unsuitable for peaceful community tanks with timid feeders or smaller fish. Confirmed distinguishing trait vs. Orchid Dottyback (lacks black eye stripe and dark purple fin edging).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Pseudochromidae, order Ovalentaria/misc), valid genus reassignment from Pseudochromis to Pictichromis, authority (Lubbock & Goldman, 1974), accepted scientific common name 'Magenta dottyback', max length 6.0 cm TL, depth range 0–65 m (commonly 0–15 m), wild temperature 25.5–29.4°C (mean 28.7°C), native distribution (Philippines to Samoa, Ryukyu Islands, Moluccas, Admiralty Islands), trophic level 3.5 (carnivore), IUCN Least Concern (2015), and habitat description (steep outer reef slopes and channel walls, solitary or small loose groups).

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk (Maidenhead Aquatics)
additional.notes: Third confirming source for max size 6 cm, water parameters (22–28°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), temperament ('fairly feisty', unsuitable with peaceful species), male territoriality, the documented jumping behavior that justifies the escapeRisk: high rating and tight-fitting cover recommendation, the small-shrimp predation caveat for reef compatibility, and a note that home-aquarium spawning has occurred (larval rearing extremely challenging).

additional.name: Reef Builders (The Magnificent Magenta Dottyback)
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size ~2.5 in TL, 20–30 US gal minimum, semi-aggressive temperament that is 'still all dottyback' but milder than congeners, high conspecific aggression toward similar-shaped fish, documented jumping incident (escape through cover gap), wild diet of small benthic crustaceans, and a caution that the species attacks more peaceful fish introduced after it becomes established.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawning species; male is generally expected to prepare a hiding site and court a female, who deposits an egg ball inside the cave that the male typically guards. Home-aquarium spawning has been reported, but larval rearing is generally considered extremely challenging due to the small larval size. Commercial captive-bred specimens exist but are less common in the trade than for some other Pseudochromidae. Pair formation in normal community-sized tanks is impractical because conspecifics cannot be safely combined.
